content: The order of the differential equation $\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}-\sin \left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)=5$
explanation:
Highest order derivative present in the given differential equation is 4 .

Thus, order of differential equation $=4$

content: The sum of the order and the degree of the differential equation $$\frac{d}{d x}\left(\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3\right)$$ is:
explanation:
$$\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^3\right]=3\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}$$

order is 2 and degree is 1

$$\therefore$$ required answer $$2+1=3$$

content:
(a) Find the particular solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y=3^{x^2} e^{x^2} ; y(0)=5$.

OR

(b) Solve the following differential equation: $$ x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 $$
explanation:
(a) Given differential equation is

$$\frac{d y}{d x}-2 x y=3 x^2 e^{x^2}$$

On comparing the above equation with

$$\frac{d y}{d x}+P y=Q$$

We get $P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}$

$$\therefore \quad I=e^{\int P d x}=e^{-\int 2 x d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =e^{-2\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)}=e^{-x^2} \\ y \cdot e^{-x^2} & =\int 3 x^2 e^{x^2} \cdot\left(e^{-x^2}\right) d x+C \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{or,}\quad y \cdot e^{-x^2} & =3 \int x^2 d x+C \\ \text{or, }\quad \frac{y}{e^{x^2}} & =3\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]+C \end{aligned}$$

or, $$\frac{y}{e^{x^2}}=x^3+C$$

or, $$y=e^{x^2} x^3+C e^{x^2}$$

Given $$y(0)=5$$

$$\therefore \quad 5=0+C e^0 \Rightarrow C=5$$

Thus, required solution is

or $$y=e^{x^2}\left(x^3+5\right)$$

OR

(b) We have,

$$\begin{aligned} x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x & =0 \\ \frac{x^2}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+(x+y) & =0 \\ \frac{x^2}{y^2} \frac{d y}{d x}+\frac{x}{y}+1 & =0\quad\text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Put }\quad y & =v x \\ \Rightarrow\quad\frac{d y}{d x} & =V+x \frac{d V}{d x} \end{aligned}$$

From eq (i), we get

$$\begin{aligned} & & \frac{1}{V^2}\left(V+x \frac{d V}{d x}\right)+\frac{1}{V}+1 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x \frac{d V}{d x} & =-\left(2 V+V^2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{V}-\frac{1}{V+2}\right] d V & =-\frac{d x}{x} \end{aligned}$$

On integrating both sides, we get

$$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}(\log V-\log V+2)=-\log x+C_1$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right)=-\log x+C_1 \\ \Rightarrow & \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right)=-2 \log x+\log k \end{array}$$

where $k=e^{2 C_1}$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & \log \left(\frac{V}{V+2}\right) & =\log \left(\frac{k}{x^2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{V}{V+2} & =\frac{k}{x^2} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $V=\frac{y}{x}$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{y}{2 x+y}=\frac{k}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2 y=C^2(2 x+y) \text { where } k=C^2 \end{aligned}$$

content:
Case Study III

An equation involving derivatives of the dependent variable with respect to the independent variables is called a differential equation. A differential equation of the form $$\frac{d y}{d x}=F(x, y)$$ is said to be homogeneous if $$\mathrm{F}(x, y)$$ is a homogeneous function of degree zero, whereas a function $$\mathrm{F}(x, y)$$ is a homogenous function of degree $$n$$ if $$\mathrm{F}(\lambda x, \lambda y)=\lambda^n F(x, y)$$. To solve a homogeneous differential equation of the type $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\mathrm{F}(x, y)= g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$, we make the substitution $$y=v x$$ and then separate the variables.

Based on the above, answer the following questions:

(i) Show that $$\left(x^2-y^2\right) d x+2 x y d x=0$$ is a differential equation of the type $$\frac{d y}{d x}=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$.

(ii) Solve the above equation to find its general solution.
explanation:
(i)

$$\begin{aligned} \left(x^2-y^2\right) d x+2 x y d y & =0 \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =-\frac{\left(x^2-y^2\right)}{2 x y} \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{y^2-x^2}{2 x y} \\ \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{x^2\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}-1\right)}{2 x y} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}-1\right)}{\frac{2 y}{x}} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=g\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)$$

(ii) $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^2-x^2}{2 x y}$$

Put $$y=v x$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}$$

$$\begin{aligned} v+x \frac{d v}{d x} & =\frac{v^2 x^2-x^2}{2 v x^2}=\frac{v^2-1}{2 v} \\ x \frac{d v}{d x} & =\frac{v^2-1}{2 v}-v \\ & =\frac{v^2-1-2 v^2}{2 v} \\ x \frac{d v}{d x} & =\frac{-\left(1+v^2\right)}{2 v} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{2 v}{1+v^2} d v & =-\frac{d x}{x} \\ \int \frac{2 v}{1+v^2} d v & =-\int \frac{d x}{x} \\ \log \left|1+v^2\right| & =-\log |x|+\log |c| \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \log \left|x\left(1+v^2\right)\right| & =\log |c| \\ x\left(1+v^2\right) & =c \\ x\left(1+\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right) & =c \\ x^2+y^2 & =c x \end{aligned}$$
